trajectory.description: Observation: Training completed 5 epochs successfully and saved checkpoints/best.pt, but predict.py crashed with a shape mismatch during normalization. Analysis: The test input has shape [B,3,2,32,64], but mean/std were viewed as [1,2,1,1] which does not broadcast to 5D (fails at dim 1 vs 3). Also normalization happened after reshaping to [B,6,32,64], which changes the channel count. Decision: Fix predict.py to view mean/std as [1,1,2,1,1] so they broadcast to the 5D input, normalize first, then reshape to [B,6,32,64]. Run a local dry-run with dummy test inputs from val_data to verify, then immediately submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline eval achieved rmse_z500=1056.0, rmse_t850=5.38 with a tiny hidden=64 U-Net trained only 5 epochs. Training is very fast (~26s/epoch). Analysis: Model is likely under-capacity and under-trained; val loss rose after epoch 2 suggesting slight overfitting that regularization can fix. Decision: Scale model to hidden=128, add dropout (0.05) and weight decay (1e-5), train up to 40 epochs with early stopping patience=7. This should significantly lower RMSE while keeping runtime well under budget.

trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline eval improved to rmse_z500=1043.3, rmse_t850=5.24 with hidden=128 U-Net, but training plateaus within 3 epochs and validation is noisy, suggesting under-capacity and possible persistence bias. Analysis: Model is tiny (~18M params) relative to 1B budget and 31GB VRAM. Weather forecasting benefits strongly from residual prediction (predict delta from persistence) and data augmentation (longitude rotation). Decision: Scale model to hidden=256 (~70-80M params), add residual connection (target = last_frame + delta), add random longitude roll augmentation, increase dropout to 0.1, train up to 60 epochs with patience=12. This should lower RMSE significantly while fitting in time budget.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4's hidden=256 U-Net trained for 21 epochs (~89 min) but val loss worsened to 0.121 vs 0.112 from iter 3, and train loss flatlined at 0.129, suggesting the U-Net with heavy downsampling (4x8 bottleneck) and skip connections is poorly suited for this forecasting task — it likely learns to persist via skip connections rather than true dynamics. Analysis: Weather forecasting benefits from full-resolution processing without destructive pooling. A deep fully-convolutional ResNet at constant 32x64 resolution with GroupNorm and a dilation schedule should preserve spatial phase and force the network to learn evolution. We should also switch to AdamW and reduce dropout. Decision: Rewrite model.py as a deep constant-resolution ResNet (12 blocks, dilation schedule 1→2), update train.py/predict.py accordingly, and run a fast 5-epoch baseline to validate the architecture and measure score/time before scaling.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 30 --residual --z500_weight 2.0 --dropout 0.1 --weight_decay 5e-4 --patience 12
trajectory.command_output_summary:
Early stopping at epoch 15 (best val 0.180204)
Done. Best val loss: 0.180204
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 5's constant-resolution ResNet achieved a new best rmse_z500=1018.6 with only 5 epochs and no residual connection, but validation loss bottomed at epoch 2 (0.1168) then rose, indicating early overfitting. Analysis: The architecture is clearly superior to the U-Net, but it needs stronger regularization, longer training, and a persistence residual (delta prediction) to generalize better. Also, since the primary metric is rmse_z500, up-weighting Z500 in the loss by 2x should help. Using mixed-precision (AMP) will cut training time by ~30-40%, letting me safely run 30 epochs within the 2.86h budget (~75-90 min training + eval). Decision: Update train.py with residual=True, channel-specific loss weights [2,1], dropout=0.1, weight_decay=5e-4, ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler, gradient clipping, and AMP. Run 30 epochs with patience=12. Keep model.py and predict.py unchanged.

Analysis: Because the loss function now up-weights Z500 by 2×, the val loss scale is not directly comparable to earlier iterations. A higher weighted loss could actually mask a significantly lower Z500 RMSE, which is our primary metric. We overwrote the previous best checkpoint, so we must evaluate this model to know whether the Z500-focused training helped. Decision: Submit evaluation for the current checkpoint immediately. This costs only ~2–3 min and is essential before deciding whether to retrain. I will also quickly inspect the checkpoint metadata.

trajectory.description: Observation: The best score so far is rmse_z500=1018.63 from iteration 5 (ResNet hidden=256, 5 epochs, no residual). Iteration 6/7 experiments with residual connections and Z500 up-weighting degraded performance to ~1075. The best checkpoint was overwritten, so we must retrain. Analysis: Given only ~85 minutes left, the most reliable way to improve upon 1018 is to train a small ensemble of diverse ResNet models (different seeds, augmentation, dropout) and apply test-time augmentation (TTA) via longitudinal rolling at inference. Ensembling + TTA should lower RMSE without requiring a brand-new architecture. Decision: Write a self-contained training script that sequentially trains 3 ResNet-256 models (5 epochs each, ~45-55 min total), saves them as a single ensemble checkpoint, and update predict.py to average the ensemble with 4-roll TTA. This should beat the previous best while fitting safely within the time budget.

The ensemble is unusable in its current form. We have ~54 min and 22 iterations left. Best prior score is rmse_z500=1018.63 from iter 5. Analysis: The ensemble is already trained and m1 achieved best_val=0.112 (better than iter 5's 0.117). Fixing the state dict keys is a 1-minute operation and could yield a strong submission immediately. We should also extract the best single model as a fallback. Decision: Write a quick script to strip '_orig_mod.' prefixes from ensemble.pt, save as ensemble_fixed.pt, and save m1 as best.pt. Run a local predict.py dry-run with the fixed ensemble to confirm it loads, then immediately submit eval for the ensemble.

If removing TTA drops local val RMSE close to or below 1018, we can simply update predict.py to disable TTA and resubmit the same checkpoint. Decision: Run a quick local evaluation on val_data with and without TTA to measure the exact impact.

The new epochs have val_loss 0.117-0.121, comparable to the iter 5 model. Analysis: A single epoch might match the prior best, but averaging weights of multiple epochs from the same training run (a model soup) can yield a more robust predictor without requiring multiple forward passes at inference. We will quickly soup epochs 1,2,4,5 (excluding the overfit epoch 3) and submit it. If time remains, we will test an individual epoch as a fallback.

trajectory.description: Observation: We have ~9.3 minutes and 10 iterations left. The best-ever eval score is rmse_z500=1018.63 from iteration 5. We have fresh per-epoch checkpoints from iteration 19 (run_epoch1-5.pt). Epoch 5 has the lowest validation loss (0.1173) among them. Since eval takes only ~20-50 seconds, we can efficiently test multiple checkpoints. Analysis: The retrained model with lower val loss (0.109) scored worse on test (1032), suggesting val-test correlation is imperfect. Therefore, testing multiple epochs from the same training run is worthwhile. Decision: Start by evaluating run_epoch5.pt. If it does not beat 1018, we will test other epochs (e.g., epoch 2 or 4) in subsequent iterations.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best-ever eval score is rmse_z500=1018.63 from iteration 5. That score was achieved with predict.py that did NOT use test-time augmentation (TTA). TTA (4 longitudinal rolls) was added in iteration 8 for ensembling and has been used in every eval since. All those later evals scored worse (1032-1145), even when validation loss was lower. We have ~9 minutes and 9 iterations remaining. Analysis: TTA is very likely harming performance because the models were not trained with longitudinal augmentation. The highest-probability path to beat 1018 is to evaluate a strong single-model checkpoint without TTA. checkpoints/best.pt is the ensemble m1 (ResNet-256, 12 blocks, 5 epochs, val_loss=0.112) — architecturally identical to the iter 5 model. Decision: Modify predict.py to disable TTA (tta_shifts=[0]), run a quick dry-run with checkpoints/best.pt to confirm it loads, then immediately submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: ~8.6 minutes and 7 iterations remain. Best-ever eval is rmse_z500=1018.63 (iter 5). Recent single-model evals without TTA (run_epoch5, best.pt, best_retrained) score 1032-1060. We have 5 epoch checkpoints (run_epoch1-5.pt) from the same training run but never evaluated them as a prediction ensemble. Analysis: Snapshot ensembling (averaging predictions across multiple training epochs) often improves generalization over any single epoch by combining different bias-variance trade-offs. It requires no additional training and inference on 5 models with batch_size=64 should finish well within time limits. Decision: Combine run_epoch1-5.pt into a single ensemble checkpoint, update predict.py to automatically ensemble all sub-models present, perform a local dry-run, and immediately submit for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: We are at iteration 24 with ~8 minutes and 6 iterations remaining. Best-ever eval score is rmse_z500=1018.63 from iteration 5. Recent single-model and ensemble evals without TTA score 1032-1060, worse than the best. The retrained model (checkpoints_retrain/best_retrained.pt) had the lowest validation loss (0.1096) but was only evaluated with TTA enabled (1032). It has never been officially evaluated without TTA. Analysis: Even without TTA, the retrained model might still not beat 1018 because validation-test correlation is imperfect. A free, no-risk improvement is to compute the mean prediction bias on the validation set and subtract it at inference time. This is guaranteed to reduce validation MSE and often helps test MSE if the climate is stable. It requires no retraining and only a few seconds of compute. Decision: Write a script to compute the per-channel/grid-cell mean residual on val_data for best_retrained.pt, save it back into the checkpoint as 'bias', update predict.py to apply the bias correction, run a dry-run, and immediately submit for evaluation. This is the highest-EV action given the extreme time constraint.

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous iteration failed because predict.py tried to reshape a [2,32,64] bias tensor into [1,2,1,1]. The best-ever eval score remains rmse_z500=1018.63 from iteration 5 (checkpoint lost). Our best available checkpoint is checkpoints_retrain/best_retrained.pt which scored 1032.17 on test. We have ~7.8 minutes and 5 iterations left. Analysis: Bias correction subtracts the mean validation residual and often reduces systematic error. The checkpoint with bias already exists (best_retrained_bias.pt); we only need to fix the shape handling in predict.py. No TTA is currently enabled, which may also help versus the original 1032 eval that likely used TTA. Decision: Fix predict.py to correctly broadcast a [2,32,64] bias tensor, run a quick dry-run, and immediately submit the bias-corrected checkpoint for evaluation. This is the highest-EV action given the extreme time constraint.

trajectory.description: Observation: We are at iteration 26 with only ~7.5 minutes remaining and 4 iterations left. The best-ever eval is rmse_z500=1018.63 from iteration 5 (checkpoint lost). Our strongest available checkpoint is best_retrained_bias.pt scoring 1026.14. Analysis: All recent single-model and ensemble attempts without TTA score 1026-1060, still above 1018. The only untapped lever is loss-function alignment: the evaluation metric uses latitude-weighted RMSE, but every model was trained with unweighted MSE. Fine-tuning the best checkpoint for just one epoch with latitude-weighted MSE can align the model with the metric and potentially shave off the remaining ~7-8 points. With batch_size=256, one epoch should take ~100 seconds, keeping total iteration time under 4 minutes and leaving a small buffer. Decision: Write a lightweight finetune.py that loads best_retrained.pt, runs one epoch of weighted MSE with low LR (1e-4), saves to finetuned.pt, dry-runs predict.py, and immediately submits for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 27's fine-tuning OOMed immediately, leaving ~7.4 min and 3 iterations. Best available score is 1026.14 (best_retrained_bias.pt, no TTA). The original 1018 checkpoint is lost. Analysis: Training is too risky now. A fast, high-EV inference-only improvement is to align bias correction with the latitude-weighted evaluation metric by computing a latitude-weighted mean residual on validation data. We also need to screen the 5 per-epoch checkpoints (run_epoch1-5.pt) with the same weighted metric to pick the best epoch for potential final evals. Decision: Write compute_weighted_bias.py to create best_retrain_weighted_bias.pt and pick_best_epoch.py to rank epochs by weighted val Z500 RMSE. Run both on CPU to avoid GPU memory issues, dry-run predict.py, and immediately submit the weighted-bias checkpoint for evaluation. This leaves two iterations for testing the best epoch if needed.
